next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The ridge trail had been climbing since dawn, and Kem's calves were filing complaints he had no language for.

In the Vault, the longest walk was sixty meters, Conservatory to east dining hall, flat stone, soft-soled shoes, silence. Nobody ran. Nobody climbed. The body was a thing you maintained through inaction, like a vase, and it performed accordingly. Now his body was being asked to carry its own weight uphill through tangled forest for six consecutive hours, and it had opinions.

Vel was thirty paces ahead, moving through the undergrowth with the ease of someone who'd done this since before he was born. She didn't look back. She hadn't looked back since they'd broken camp, and Kem had stopped deciding whether that meant trust or indifference because both answers felt equally bad.

The highland forest closed overhead. The trees were old. Kem couldn't tell one from another, an omission in his education he had not known existed until two days ago. He'd asked Vel what a particular tree was called, and she'd looked at him as if he'd asked what walking was. So he had stopped asking. Light filtered through the canopy in shifting columns. The air smelled of earth, resin, and leaves that had been falling toward soil since last year. Pack straps had worn through the linen of his shirt and were working on the skin beneath. By afternoon he would have fresh blisters. Yesterday supplied precedent.

His Ledger read 286, a number he felt the way he'd once felt the Vault's walls, always there at the edge of thought, a quiet hum of remaining capacity. Fourteen levels had go...
open_hooks_to_carry: Vel's disgust at Kem's wastefulness — 286 after mere days
The terms: she guides, he follows, she controls information flow
What Vel conceals: the mechanism's specific requirement for a high-level spender
prev_tail_excerpt:
...thing else with a better claim on seven levels' worth of lunch. South was the direction the path offered, so south he went.

The pine forest thinned. Trees gave way to scrubby grassland. Shade gave way to full sun. Heat hit him clean and merciless the moment the last pines fell behind. His neck was already pink. His face felt tight. Out here the air tasted drier, full of dust and pollen, nothing like the Vault's managed atmosphere. At the edge of awareness the Ledger kept humming, quieter now by twelve points. The sensation was hard to name, a fullness slightly less full, like a room with a few books pulled from the shelf. Kem barely noticed it.

South stayed south. His body did what it could, which was less than he would have liked and more than he had any right to expect from a body that had spent twenty-six years doing nothing. Feet bled. Shoulders ached from the unfamiliar rhythm of swinging his arms. Sun caught the signet and flashed. Kem looked at the Aldraste crest, the closed circle, the symbol of a House built on the principle that the best use of power was no use at all, and kept walking. Every few steps the thought of stopping came up. Each time he saw the Conservatory bench, his father's open eyes, and chose the road instead.

Behind him the food cooled in the pine forest. The fox ate until it could not move and lay on its side near the roast. Ants disassembled the cake tier by tier. Stew thickened in its bowls. Bread went stale. Back at the Vault, the gate remained open because no one inside had yet convened a committee to determine whether it should be closed.

setups_to_plant.description: Vel's careful assessment of Kem's remaining levels and her private satisfaction that he solves her problem — reads as pragmatic alliance-building, later revealed as calculated manipulation

state_deltas_required: Mutual need and mutual disgust between two people who represent opposite failures of the Ledger system
Uneasy alliance formed; Vel has knowledge, Kem has levels, neither trusts the other
